condition_overview.summary: Insomnia Disorder is characterised by persistent difficulty initiating or maintaining sleep, or early morning awakening with inability to return to sleep, occurring at least three nights per week for at least three months, causing clinically significant daytime impairment. It is classified as either primary or comorbid (secondary) and is strongly associated with hyperarousal of the central nervous system. Cognitive Behavioural Therapy for Insomnia (CBT-I) remains the first-line treatment; neuromodulation is adjunctive or for cases where CBT-I is insufficient.
condition_overview.prevalence: Epidemiologic estimates depend heavily on whether studies count insomnia symptoms versus insomnia disorder and which criteria are used. Narrative reviews and population surveys commonly report chronic insomnia disorder in roughly single-digit to low–double-digit percentages of adults, with higher rates of occasional symptoms; women, older adults, and people with psychiatric or medical comorbidity are often over-represented.

phenotype_clusters.name: Sleep Onset Difficulty
phenotype_clusters.description: Primary difficulty falling asleep at desired bedtime. Characterised by prolonged sleep onset latency (>30 min), often linked to hyperarousal, anxiety, and conditioned arousal in bed. Common in younger adults.
phenotype_clusters.qeeg_signature: Elevated beta at frontal sites during pre-sleep period; reduced alpha at Cz near sleep onset

phenotype_clusters.name: Maintenance Insomnia
phenotype_clusters.description: Difficulty staying asleep — frequent nocturnal awakenings lasting >20 minutes with difficulty returning to sleep. Associated with increased arousal threshold and fragmented sleep architecture. More common in older adults.
phenotype_clusters.qeeg_signature: Fragmented sleep spindles; reduced slow-wave activity (delta); increased high-frequency EEG during NREM

phenotype_clusters.name: Hyperarousal Insomnia
phenotype_clusters.description: Central hyperarousal as the predominant mechanism — elevated physiological, cognitive, and cortical arousal persisting into sleep-onset period. Often co-occurs with anxiety. Characterised by racing thoughts, inability to 'switch off', and somatic tension.
phenotype_clusters.qeeg_signature: Elevated frontal beta (20–30 Hz) especially at F3/F4; elevated right DLPFC activation during pre-sleep

phenotype_clusters.name: Comorbid Anxiety-Insomnia
phenotype_clusters.description: Insomnia occurring in the context of a diagnosed anxiety disorder (GAD, panic disorder, social anxiety) where anxiety-driven hyperarousal is the primary driver of sleep disruption. Requires joint anxiety and sleep management.
phenotype_clusters.qeeg_signature: Elevated frontal beta; reduced alpha; high-frequency EEG bursts during relaxation attempts

phenotype_clusters.name: Comorbid Depression-Insomnia
phenotype_clusters.description: Insomnia co-occurring with major depressive disorder, characterised by early morning awakening, reduced slow-wave sleep, shortened REM latency, and mood-driven rumination. Requires treating both conditions concurrently.
phenotype_clusters.qeeg_signature: Right frontal alpha asymmetry; reduced delta power; abnormal REM onset

symptom_clusters: Sleep onset difficulty (prolonged sleep onset latency >30 min)
Sleep maintenance difficulty (frequent or prolonged nocturnal awakenings)
Early morning awakening (waking ≥30 min before desired time, unable to return to sleep)
Daytime impairment (fatigue, concentration difficulty, mood disturbance, functional impairment)
Cognitive difficulties related to poor sleep (attention, memory, decision-making)
Emotional consequences of poor sleep (irritability, mood lability, anxiety about sleep)
condition_overview.neurobiology: Contemporary models emphasise conditioned arousal, cognitive/hyperarousal, circadian and homeostatic dysregulation, and heterogeneity across individuals. Polysomnographic and EEG findings vary; elevated beta or altered sigma/spindle measures may be seen in some patients but are not diagnostic. Proposed brain targets for adjunctive neuromodulation (e.g., sensorimotor rhythm training at Cz; prefrontal tDCS montages) are mechanistic hypotheses supported by small trials at best — not established pathways like CBT-I.
comorbidities: Generalised anxiety disorder (commonly co-occurs with insomnia)
Major depressive disorder (commonly co-occurs with insomnia)
Obstructive sleep apnoea (frequently co-occurring; must be ruled out before neuromodulation)
ADHD (delayed sleep phase, difficulty with sleep initiation)
Restless legs syndrome / periodic limb movements
differential_diagnoses: Obstructive sleep apnoea (fragmented sleep due to airway obstruction — polysomnography required)
Restless legs syndrome (uncomfortable limb sensations delaying sleep onset)
Circadian rhythm sleep-wake disorder (delayed or advanced sleep phase — actigraphy recommended)
Periodic limb movement disorder (limb movements causing arousals)
Parasomnias (sleep terrors, sleepwalking, REM sleep behaviour disorder)
Primary hypersomnia / idiopathic hypersomnia (excessive daytime sleepiness rather than night-time insomnia)
Mood disorder (depression with hypersomnia or anxiety with nocturnal arousal)
Medication-induced sleep disturbance (stimulants, SSRIs, corticosteroids, beta-blockers)

patient_friendly_explanation.what_is_it: Insomnia is when you regularly have trouble falling asleep, staying asleep, or waking up too early and not being able to get back to sleep. This happens even when you have enough time and opportunity for sleep, and it leaves you feeling tired and not functioning well during the day.
patient_friendly_explanation.how_it_affects_you: Poor sleep affects almost every part of life. You may feel exhausted but still unable to sleep. You might find it hard to concentrate at work, feel more irritable or low in mood, and worry more about your sleep as time goes on — which can make the problem worse. Over time, chronic insomnia can affect your physical health, memory, and emotional wellbeing.
patient_friendly_explanation.treatment_options_overview: Clinical practice guidelines (for example the American Academy of Sleep Medicine) strongly recommend multicomponent CBT-I as a first-line treatment for chronic insomnia in adults. Some people also explore adjunctive devices. Cranial electrotherapy stimulation (CES) devices may be marketed with regulatory clearance in some regions; clinical trial evidence summarized in a 2018 VA systematic review was insufficient to conclude clinically important effects on insomnia specifically, despite device availability. Neurofeedback approaches are not first-line and remain adjunctive/experimental for many patients.
patient_friendly_explanation.what_to_expect: CES treatment involves wearing small ear clip electrodes for about 60 minutes each evening before bed. It is painless and many people find it relaxing. Neurofeedback involves attending clinic sessions 2 times per week for several weeks, where sensors on your head provide real-time feedback about your brain activity. Your clinician will track your sleep quality using questionnaires throughout your treatment.

faq.question: Is CBT-I really necessary — can't I just use the brain stimulation?
faq.answer: CBT-I is the most effective treatment for chronic insomnia and should be offered alongside any neuromodulation approach. Brain stimulation and neurofeedback work best when combined with good sleep habits and any thought patterns about sleep that CBT-I addresses. Your clinician will discuss both options with you.

faq.question: Will I become dependent on the CES device to sleep?
faq.answer: CES is designed to be used during a defined treatment course. The goal is to reset your brain's sleep patterns so that you can sleep more naturally over time. Unlike sleeping tablets, there is no pharmacological dependence associated with CES devices.

faq.question: How long will the treatment take?
faq.answer: CES is typically used nightly for 3–5 weeks. Neurofeedback courses are usually 20–30 sessions over 10–15 weeks. Your sleep should gradually improve over the course. Some people notice improvements within 1–2 weeks; others need the full course.

faq.question: What about sleeping tablets — should I stop them?
faq.answer: Do not stop any prescribed medication without talking to your doctor first. Your clinician and prescriber will advise on how to manage your sleep medication alongside neuromodulation. CES treatment is not a substitute for medically indicated treatment.

faq.question: Are there any risks?
faq.answer: CES devices are generally considered low risk when used as directed, and many people only notice mild tingling. Regulatory clearance (for example FDA device clearance in the U.S.) reflects safety/marketing status for a device class; it does not guarantee you will benefit. Neurofeedback is non-invasive but requires sessions and training. Your clinician will review your history and discuss realistic expectations.

required_fields.field: Confirmed insomnia diagnosis (DSM-5 or ICD-10 criteria documented)
required_fields.reason: Protocol generation requires a confirmed diagnosis. Duration (≥3 months), frequency (≥3 nights/week), and daytime impairment must be documented.

required_fields.field: Insomnia subtype (onset difficulty, maintenance, early morning awakening, mixed)
required_fields.reason: Subtype determines primary phenotype and protocol selection.

required_fields.field: Sleep apnoea screen or polysomnography result
required_fields.reason: Obstructive sleep apnoea is a major differential and comorbidity that must be ruled out before insomnia-specific neuromodulation. Untreated sleep apnoea is a contraindication to CES ear clips if significant nasal/airway compromise.
CBT-I is first-line for insomnia. Documentation of CBT-I offer or completion is required before protocol generation. If CBT-I is contraindicated or refused, document reason.

required_fields.field: Current and past sleep medications (name, dose, duration, response)
required_fields.reason: Sedative-hypnotics, Z-drugs, melatonin, and antihistamines affect baseline sleep architecture and must be documented for protocol adjustment and interaction screening.

required_fields.field: Psychiatric comorbidity screen (anxiety, depression, PTSD, ADHD)
required_fields.reason: Psychiatric comorbidities drive insomnia phenotype selection and affect protocol choice. Must be documented and concurrent treatment planned.

required_fields.field: Restless legs syndrome / periodic limb movements screen
required_fields.reason: RLS/PLMD is a common comorbidity and differential that requires its own treatment — must be documented before insomnia neuromodulation.
CES requires ear clip electrode placement; cardiac pacemaker is a relative contraindication. tDCS contraindicated with implanted cardiac devices.

required_fields.field: Active ear infections or skin lesions at ear placement sites
required_fields.reason: Active ear conditions contraindicate CES ear clip electrodes.

required_fields.field: Scalp and skin integrity at electrode sites
required_fields.reason: Open wounds or active dermatitis at electrode sites contraindicate tDCS.

clinical_interview_checklist: Onset and duration of insomnia symptoms (months/years)
Precipitating events (life stressor, illness, medication change, bereavement)
Typical sleep pattern: bedtime, time to fall asleep, night awakenings, wake time, total sleep time
Sleep diary review (at least 2 weeks prior to intake if available)
Daytime consequences: fatigue, concentration, mood, work/social functioning
Worry about sleep and catastrophic thinking (sleep-related cognitions)
Pre-sleep arousal behaviours (screen time, caffeine, exercise timing, bedroom environment)
Substance use: caffeine intake, alcohol use (initial sedation then arousal disruption), cannabis
Previous insomnia treatments: CBT-I, sleep restriction, sleep hygiene, medications — response and reason for discontinuation
Shift work or irregular schedules affecting sleep timing
Partner-reported observations: snoring, witnessed apnoeas, limb movements (screen for OSA/PLMD)
Motivation and expectations for neuromodulation treatment
rule_out_differentials: Obstructive sleep apnoea (particularly if snoring, witnessed apnoeas, obesity, or excessive daytime sleepiness — refer for polysomnography)
Restless legs syndrome (uncomfortable limb sensations worse at rest and evening — clinical interview or actigraphy)
Circadian rhythm disorder — delayed or advanced sleep phase (consistent pattern of inability to sleep at desired time but normal sleep at shifted schedule)
Periodic limb movement disorder (nocturnal limb movements causing arousals — polysomnography if suspected)
Medication-induced insomnia (SSRIs, SNRIs, stimulants, corticosteroids, beta-blockers — review medication list)
Substance-induced sleep disorder (alcohol dependence, cannabis withdrawal, stimulant misuse)
Hyperthyroidism (tremor, heat intolerance, anxiety, insomnia — TSH screen if suspected)
Primary mood or anxiety disorder where insomnia is a symptom rather than the primary condition
comorbidity_screen: Depression (PHQ-9 — scores ≥10 require concurrent depression treatment before or alongside insomnia protocols)
Generalised anxiety (GAD-7 — anxiety-driven hyperarousal affects protocol phenotype selection)
PTSD (PCL-5 — nightmares and hypervigilance require trauma-specific treatment alongside insomnia management)
Daytime sleepiness (ESS — excessive daytime sleepiness may indicate comorbid sleep disorder rather than pure insomnia)
Chronic pain (BPI or NRS — pain-disrupted sleep requires pain management as primary or co-treatment)
prior_treatment_required: CBT-I (individual or group) — must be offered and documentation of offer or completion required
Stimulus control and sleep restriction therapy
Relaxation training (progressive muscle relaxation, guided imagery)
Pharmacological management review by prescriber if long-term hypnotic use

additional_requirements: Comprehensive medication review (polypharmacy is common and contributes to insomnia and fall risk)
Cognitive screen (MoCA or MMSE) to ensure capacity for neurofeedback task engagement
Fall risk assessment before home CES use (nighttime use, trip hazard from cable)
Adjust session parameters for potential increased skin sensitivity (reduce tDCS intensity)
CES: Insufficient safety data in pregnancy — defer to obstetric review
tDCS: Insufficient safety data — not recommended without obstetric clearance
Neurofeedback: No known foetal risk; preferred option in pregnancy
Document obstetric status at every session review

absolute.condition: Active epilepsy or uncontrolled seizure disorder
absolute.rationale: CES and tDCS alter cortical excitability and may lower seizure threshold. All electrical stimulation protocols are contraindicated until seizure disorder is controlled and neurologist clearance obtained.
CES ear clip electrodes and tDCS current may interfere with pacemaker function. Absolute contraindication for CES and tDCS. Neurofeedback may be considered with cardiologist input.
Insomnia-specific neuromodulation will not address the primary cause of sleep disruption in OSA. Treating insomnia without addressing OSA may delay necessary CPAP treatment and cause patient harm.

absolute.condition: Active ear infection or open skin lesion at ear clip electrode sites (CES)
absolute.rationale: CES ear clip placement directly contacts ear tissue; active infection or open wounds risk infection spread and tissue damage.
Neuromodulation is contraindicated until psychiatric stabilisation is achieved. Insomnia treatment must not take precedence over acute psychiatric emergency management.

relative.rationale: Insomnia in the context of untreated severe depression may reflect a symptom of the primary mood disorder. CES and neurofeedback alone will be insufficient.
relative.mitigation: Initiate depression treatment concurrently. Insomnia neuromodulation may augment depression treatment. Review at 4 weeks. Document concurrent treatment plan.

relative.condition: Severe anxiety disorder not in treatment
relative.rationale: Anxiety-driven hyperarousal is a primary driver of insomnia in this phenotype. Neuromodulation without anxiety treatment may have limited effectiveness.
relative.mitigation: Initiate anxiety treatment (pharmacological or CBT) before or concurrently. CES may be discussed as an adjunct; evidence quality should not be overstated. Document joint treatment plan.

relative.rationale: Chronic hypnotic use alters baseline sleep architecture and cortical excitability, making baseline sleep assessments unreliable and neuromodulation response harder to predict.
relative.mitigation: Coordinate with prescriber for planned tapering alongside neuromodulation. Document baseline under current medication. Do not abruptly discontinue — risk of rebound insomnia and seizure.

relative.condition: Skin lesion, eczema, or dermatitis at electrode placement site
relative.rationale: Active skin conditions at electrode sites increase risk of skin irritation or chemical burn under tDCS electrodes.
relative.mitigation: Delay tDCS until skin healed. CES ear clip electrodes may be used at unaffected sites. Alternative electrode placement if clinically feasible.

relative.rationale: CES and tDCS have insufficient safety data in pregnancy; insufficient evidence for foetal safety.
relative.mitigation: Neurofeedback is the preferred option with no known foetal risk. Obtain obstetric clearance for CES or tDCS. Document in record.

special_populations.flag: Increased skin fragility — monitor electrode sites closely. Cognitive capacity may limit engagement with neurofeedback tasks. Fall risk assessment required for home CES use.
Pain-related sleep disruption may not respond adequately to insomnia-only protocols. Pain management must be addressed concurrently.

ces: Do not use if active ear infection present
Do not use with implanted cardiac device (pacemaker, defibrillator, ICD)
Do not use over open skin wounds at ear clip sites
Remove all metal ear jewellery before electrode placement
Do not use while operating heavy machinery or driving
tdcs: No open wounds or skin lesions at electrode sites (F4/Fp1 or equivalent)
No metal skull implants at stimulation site
Remove all head and neck metal jewellery before session
Do not proceed if recent scalp surgery
neurofeedback: No absolute contraindications to neurofeedback for insomnia
Active psychosis is a relative contraindication — neurofeedback not appropriate during acute psychotic episode
Ensure adequate scalp condition for gel electrode contact

screening.name: ISI
screening.rationale: Gold-standard 7-item validated insomnia screener and severity measure. Score 0–7: no insomnia; 8–14: subthreshold; 15–21: moderate; 22–28: severe. Primary severity instrument for protocol selection and monitoring. Free and widely validated across languages.

screening.name: ESS
screening.rationale: 8-item scale assessing daytime sleepiness across common situations. Score >10 suggests excessive daytime sleepiness, which may indicate comorbid sleep disorder (OSA, narcolepsy) requiring investigation before insomnia-specific treatment.

diagnostic.name: PSQI
diagnostic.rationale: 19-item validated measure of sleep quality over the past month covering 7 component scores: subjective quality, latency, duration, efficiency, disturbances, medication use, and daytime dysfunction. PSQI >5 indicates poor sleep quality. Captures sleep architecture beyond insomnia severity alone.

diagnostic.name: Sleep Diary (2-week)
diagnostic.rationale: Prospective 2-week sleep diary provides objective daily data on sleep onset latency, wake after sleep onset, total sleep time, and sleep efficiency. Essential for insomnia diagnosis confirmation per DSM-5 criteria and for establishing individualised baseline.
diagnostic.access: Free (Consensus Sleep Diary available at consensussleepdiary.com)

baseline.name: ISI (Baseline)
baseline.rationale: Primary outcome baseline score. All subsequent monitoring and response thresholds are referenced against this score.

baseline.name: PSQI (Baseline)
baseline.rationale: Captures baseline multidimensional sleep quality for comparison at end of course. Documents sleep efficiency, duration, and medication use at intake.

baseline.name: PHQ-9
baseline.rationale: Screens for comorbid depression and quantifies severity. Scores ≥10 indicate moderate depression requiring concurrent treatment. Item 9 monitors suicidality. Required at baseline for all insomnia protocols.

baseline.name: GAD-7
baseline.rationale: Screens for comorbid anxiety disorder. Elevated GAD-7 identifies anxiety-driven hyperarousal phenotype, directing protocol selection toward hyperarousal-targeted approaches (tDCS right DLPFC, CES).

monitoring.name: ISI (Monitoring)
monitoring.rationale: Primary monitoring instrument. Administered every 5 sessions (or every 2 weeks for CES home programme). Change from baseline is the primary response signal.

monitoring.name: Sleep Diary (Ongoing)
monitoring.full_name: Consensus Sleep Diary — Ongoing Weekly Record
monitoring.rationale: Continuous daily sleep tracking allows real-time detection of treatment response and adverse changes (increased arousal, rebound insomnia). Weekly clinic review of diary data during active treatment.

outcome.name: ISI (End of Course)
outcome.rationale: Primary outcome measure. Response defined as ≥8-point reduction from baseline ISI score. Remission defined as ISI score <8 (sub-threshold or no insomnia range). Clinically meaningful change benchmark: ≥8 points per Morin et al. 2011.

outcome.name: PSQI (End of Course)
outcome.rationale: Secondary outcome capturing multidimensional sleep quality change. Response: PSQI total score reduction ≥3 points from baseline. Remission: PSQI ≤5.

neurophysiological.name: qEEG Sleep Assessment
neurophysiological.full_name: Quantitative EEG — Insomnia Biomarker Assessment (Pre-Sleep Beta, SMR at Cz)
neurophysiological.rationale: Documents cortical hyperarousal (elevated beta 20–30 Hz at Cz/Fz), reduced SMR (12–15 Hz), and frontal beta asymmetry relevant to phenotype selection and neurofeedback protocol personalisation. Optional but recommended for neurofeedback protocols.
neurophysiological.access: Requires qEEG system and certified interpreter

functional.name: ESS (Endpoint)
functional.full_name: Epworth Sleepiness Scale — Functional Daytime Impact
functional.rationale: Measures functional impact of sleep quality on daytime alertness and activity. Improvement in ESS confirms functional benefit of treatment beyond symptom score reduction.

protocol_bundle.name: CES Alpha-Stim — Cranial Electrotherapy Stimulation for Insomnia (On-label)
protocol_bundle.evidence_summary: U.S. FDA Class II cranial electrotherapy stimulator devices (including commonly used insomnia indications on labeling) are available, but regulatory clearance is not equivalent to large-effect, low-bias efficacy evidence. A 2018 VA Evidence Synthesis Program systematic review (Shekelle et al., NCBI Bookshelf) concluded evidence was insufficient to support conclusions that CES has clinically important effects on insomnia (and several other endpoints), with most RCTs at high risk of bias; it reported low-strength evidence for possible modest benefit mainly in anxiety with depression, and CES appeared largely free of serious adverse events in available reports. Narrative reviews (e.g., Kirsch & Nichols, 2014, Psychiatr Clin North Am) summarize manufacturer-associated and older trials — interpret cautiously. Mechanistic statements should remain tentative.
references: Shekelle PG, Cook I, Miake-Lye IM, et al. The Effectiveness and Risks of Cranial Electrical Stimulation for the Treatment of Pain, Depression, Anxiety, PTSD, and Insomnia: A Systematic Review. Washington (DC): Department of Veterans Affairs (US); 2018 Feb. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK493132/
Kirsch DL, Nichols F. Cranial electrotherapy stimulation for treatment of anxiety, depression, and insomnia. Psychiatr Clin North Am. 2014;37(1):131-168.

brain_target.region: Bilateral cortical and subcortical networks via transcranial low-level current

parameters.intensity: 100 µA (starting); adjustable up to 500 µA based on patient tolerance
parameters.electrode_placement: Bilateral ear clip electrodes (Alpha-Stim clip electrodes on ear lobes bilaterally)

additional_params.session_timing: 60 minutes nightly, within 1 hour of intended bedtime
additional_params.home_use: Yes — patient-operated at home after clinic training session

session_structure.maintenance_sessions: Optional: 3–5 nights per week maintenance use if symptoms recur after course completion

steps.title: Device setup and electrode preparation
steps.detail: Apply conductive solution to ear clip electrode felt pads. Clip to ear lobes bilaterally. Connect leads to device. Confirm no skin lesions at ear sites. Remove metal ear jewellery.

steps.title: Current setting and ramp-up
steps.detail: Set to 0.5 Hz, starting at 100 µA. Instruct patient to feel gentle tingling — if uncomfortable reduce to minimum perceptible level. Increase slowly toward 100 µA if well tolerated.

steps.title: Active CES session
steps.detail: Patient rests quietly (lying down or seated in a comfortable position). May listen to relaxation audio or practice slow breathing. Avoid screens during session. Device delivers 0.5 Hz biphasic current.

steps.title: Post-session log
steps.detail: Patient records session in sleep diary: session time, comfort rating, subjective relaxation level. Reports to clinic at weekly check-in.

monitoring_required: ISI every 5 sessions (weekly for home CES)
Sleep diary updated daily throughout course
Skin check at ear clip sites at each clinic visit or patient self-report weekly
PHQ-9 at baseline and end of course
protocol_bundle.adverse_event_monitoring: Common: mild tingling at ear clip sites (expected — reassure patient). Rare: skin irritation or redness at ear clip site, mild headache, dizziness. Very rare: transient phosphene (brief visual flash — reduce intensity). Report any persistent headache, skin wound, or unexpected neurological symptoms immediately.
escalation_rules: ISI reduction <8 points after 3 weeks: clinician review; consider adding neurofeedback SMR training or tDCS; assess CBT-I engagement
Persistent skin irritation at ear sites on 2+ consecutive days: cease CES, inspect skin, consider alternative electrode pad material
New onset of suicidal ideation (PHQ-9 item 9): immediate clinician review; treat depression concurrently

governance.notes: Device labeling (on-label vs off-label) differs from strength of clinical trial evidence. Clinician sign-off required given VA 2018 systematic review conclusions. Document CBT-I discussion/referral per AASM-style practice. Home use only after screening and training.

protocol_bundle.notes: Use as an adjunct after documenting discussion of first-line CBT-I per contemporary sleep-medicine guidelines. Trial evidence for CES in insomnia is not robust in independent systematic review — track outcomes with ISI/sleep diary. Verify manufacturer IFU for device-specific parameters.

protocol_bundle.name: Neurofeedback — SMR Enhancement for Insomnia (Off-label, Emerging)
protocol_bundle.evidence_summary: Sensorimotor rhythm (SMR, 12–15 Hz) neurofeedback at Cz enhances sleep spindle activity, improves NREM sleep architecture, and reduces sleep onset latency in uncontrolled and controlled studies (Sterman & Friar, 1972; Cortoos et al., 2010; Schabus et al., 2014). RCTs are limited in sample size and methodological quality. Current evidence supports EV-C (emerging evidence from controlled studies, not yet multiple RCTs with blinding). Rationale is well-grounded in sleep neuroscience: SMR enhancement at Cz correlates with sigma (sleep spindle) production, promoting sleep stage N2 consolidation.
references: Sterman MB & Friar L (1972). Suppression of seizures in an epileptic following sensorimotor EEG feedback training. Electroencephalography and Clinical Neurophysiology, 33(1), 89–95.

Cortoos A et al. (2010). An Exploratory Study on the Effects of Tele-Neurofeedback and Tele-Biofeedback on Objective and Subjective Sleep in Patients with Primary Insomnia. Applied Psychophysiology and Biofeedback, 35(2), 125–134.
Schabus M et al. (2014). Enhancing Sleep Quality and Memory in Insomnia Using Instrumental Sensorimotor Rhythm Conditioning. Biological Psychology, 95, 126–134.

parameters.reward_band: SMR 12–15 Hz (sigma range — promotes sleep spindle production)
parameters.inhibit_band: Theta 4–8 Hz (reduce daytime theta leak into pre-sleep period)
parameters.feedback_modality: Audio + visual (calm visual animation paired with reward tone)
parameters.electrode_sites: Active: Cz; Reference: A1 or A2 (mastoid); Ground: Fpz

session_structure.maintenance_sessions: Booster sessions (4–6 over 2–3 weeks) if ISI score increases ≥4 points at 3-month follow-up

steps.title: Scalp preparation and electrode placement
steps.detail: Clean Cz site with NuPrep abrasive gel. Apply conductive paste (Ten20). Apply electrode and check impedance <5 kΩ. Apply mastoid references and Fpz ground.

steps.title: Baseline EEG (eyes closed)
steps.detail: Record 3 minutes eyes closed baseline EEG. Assess resting SMR/theta ratio. Set reward threshold based on baseline.

steps.title: SMR neurofeedback training
steps.detail: 4–5 runs of 5 minutes each with rest breaks. Patient receives audio-visual reward when SMR (12–15 Hz) exceeds adaptive threshold. Inhibit theta. Use calm, low-stimulus visual environment to promote pre-sleep-like state. Sessions ideally in late afternoon to support sleep drive.

steps.title: Post-session review
steps.detail: Record reward rate, threshold crossings, and signal quality metrics. Ask patient to rate pre-session and post-session sleepiness (KSS). Log any adverse events or unusual experiences.

monitoring_required: Sleep diary reviewed weekly throughout course
Session-level sleepiness rating (Karolinska Sleepiness Scale) pre and post each session
qEEG SMR/theta ratio remeasurement at session 10 (optional but recommended)
protocol_bundle.adverse_event_monitoring: Monitor for: transient post-session fatigue or drowsiness (common; advise against driving immediately after late-day sessions), mood changes, increased arousal in rare cases. Log any worsening of insomnia or new anxiety symptoms.
escalation_rules: ISI reduction <4 points after 10 sessions: review electrode placement, consider frontal variant (Fz SMR) or switching to CES protocol
Patient reports worsened insomnia or increased arousal after sessions: reassess session timing (avoid evening sessions), reduce session length, consider alternative protocol
New anxiety or depressive symptoms emerging: concurrent mental health assessment required

governance.notes: EV-C. Off-label. Clinician sign-off required before protocol generation. Patient export permitted after sign-off. Off-label acknowledgement required. CBT-I must be documented as offered.

protocol_bundle.notes: Best suited to maintenance insomnia and sleep architecture disruption phenotypes. Schedule sessions in afternoon rather than evening to avoid post-session drowsiness while driving. Rationale is strong (SMR-spindle coupling) but more robust RCT evidence is needed. Always combine with CBT-I.

protocol_bundle.name: tDCS — Cathodal Right DLPFC for Hyperarousal Insomnia (Off-label, Emerging)
protocol_bundle.evidence_summary: This protocol uses cathodal right DLPFC tDCS as a **hypothesis-based, off-label** approach for hyperarousal-predominant insomnia. Prior references embedded here were incorrect (unrelated animal/creativity studies) and have been removed. Human insomnia tDCS literature includes small pilots and newer trials using **different montages/stimulation types** (for example high-definition tDCS targeting medial prefrontal regions). Li et al. (2025, Sci Rep) reported a randomized sham-controlled trial of HD-tDCS in chronic insomnia with PSQI and PSG changes — **this does not validate the specific cathodal R-DLPFC conventional montage in this template.** Evidence grade remains EV-C: exploratory, clinician-directed, not standard care.
references: Li J, Li A, Jiao J, et al. Effects of high-definition transcranial direct current stimulation for the treatment of chronic insomnia: a randomized, double-blind, controlled trial. Sci Rep. 2025. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-025-09304-4

parameters.electrode_placement: Cathode: F4 (right DLPFC); Anode: left supraorbital (Fp1) or left shoulder (extra-cephalic)

additional_params.session_timing: Administered in early evening (2–3 hours before intended bedtime)
additional_params.skin_prep: Clean with isopropyl alcohol; assess skin integrity before each session

session_structure.maintenance_sessions: Reassess at 6 weeks; booster course of 5 sessions if ISI score increases ≥6 points

steps.title: Pre-session checks
steps.detail: Confirm skin integrity at F4 and Fp1. Remove metal jewellery from head and neck. Screen for new contraindications. Verify patient has not consumed alcohol.

steps.title: Electrode placement
steps.detail: Position cathode (blue lead) saline sponge at F4 (right DLPFC). Position anode at Fp1 or left deltoid (extra-cephalic preferred to minimise eye discomfort). Secure with elastic headband.

steps.title: Active stimulation
steps.detail: 30-second ramp-up to 1.5 mA. Patient may practise slow breathing or mindfulness during stimulation. Advise patient to remain seated quietly. Monitor for tingling, headache, or phosphene.

steps.title: Ramp-down and post-session review
steps.detail: 30-second ramp-down. Inspect electrode sites. Log subjective arousal rating (1–10) pre and post. Document any adverse sensations.

monitoring_required: ISI at baseline, session 5, and end of course
Subjective arousal rating (1–10) pre and post every session
Skin integrity check at electrode sites before every session
PHQ-9 and GAD-7 at baseline and end of course
protocol_bundle.adverse_event_monitoring: Common: mild tingling or itching at electrode sites (expected). Occasional: scalp redness (resolve within 1 hour). Rare: headache, transient phosphene if anode near eye (use extra-cephalic placement). Serious: skin burn (incorrect electrode contact or impedance failure — inspect after every session).
escalation_rules: ISI reduction <4 points after 10 sessions: clinician review; reassess phenotype; switch to or add CES protocol; review CBT-I engagement
Persistent skin redness >1 hour: reassess electrode placement and current density; reduce to 1 mA
Worsening anxiety or depressive scores: concurrent mental health review; consider switching to CES which addresses both anxiety and insomnia

governance.notes: EV-C off-label tDCS. Clinician sign-off required. Patient export blocked. Off-label acknowledgement mandatory. Restricted to hyperarousal insomnia phenotype. CBT-I must be offered concurrently.

protocol_bundle.notes: Restricted to the hyperarousal insomnia phenotype (PHE-INS-03) with elevated GAD-7 and documented pre-sleep cognitive arousal. Not for routine insomnia use. Should be offered only after CES has been trialled or is contraindicated. Session timing is critical — early evening only.

device_options.name: Alpha-Stim AID
device_options.cleared_indication: U.S. FDA Class II cranial electrotherapy stimulator devices are typically cleared via 510(k) substantial equivalence; confirm insomnia/anxiety/depression indications and contraindications on the manufacturer labeling for the exact model issued.
constraints: Patient training session required before home use
Ear clip electrodes must be replaced after recommended use cycles
Do not use with implanted cardiac devices
Do not use while operating machinery or driving
device_options.notes: Confirm prescription/status and IFU for your jurisdiction. Efficacy evidence should be discussed using independent systematic reviews, not device marketing alone.

device_options.name: BrainMaster Atlantis II
device_options.cleared_indication: FDA 510(k) cleared as biofeedback device; specific insomnia indication not cleared
constraints: Requires certified neurofeedback practitioner (BCIA certification recommended)
Clinical-grade EEG system required for SMR protocol accuracy
device_options.notes: Standard clinical neurofeedback system for SMR insomnia protocol. Supports real-time sigma/SMR reward at Cz.

device_options.name: Soterix Medical 1×1 tDCS Device
device_options.cleared_indication: FDA-cleared for clinical research; no insomnia clearance
constraints: Protocol uses maximum 1.5 mA — device supports up to 4 mA
device_options.notes: Research-grade tDCS device. Consistent current delivery and ramp control. Suitable for right DLPFC cathodal hyperarousal protocol.

brain_targets.rationale: The vertex (Cz) is the primary neurofeedback target for insomnia. SMR (12–15 Hz) generated at the sensorimotor cortex correlates directly with sleep spindle production (sigma band, 12–15 Hz) during NREM stage N2. Enhancing SMR through neurofeedback promotes deeper, more consolidated sleep and reduces sleep onset latency. This target is supported by the earliest animal and human neurofeedback sleep research.
brain_targets.qeeg_target: Increase SMR (12–15 Hz); decrease theta (4–8 Hz) — promotes sleep spindle architecture
brain_targets.evidence_note: SMR-sleep spindle coupling is well-established in sleep neuroscience. Neurofeedback SMR enhancement for insomnia supported by Schabus et al. 2014 and Cortoos et al. 2010.

brain_targets.rationale: Elevated right DLPFC activity is associated with pre-sleep cognitive hyperarousal, worry, and rumination — primary drivers of hyperarousal insomnia phenotype. Cathodal tDCS reduces right DLPFC excitability, attenuating the hyperarousal-insomnia cycle. Additionally, right DLPFC inhibitory effects may reduce anxiety-driven pre-sleep arousal.
brain_targets.qeeg_target: Reduce right frontal high-frequency beta (20–30 Hz) activity during pre-sleep period
brain_targets.evidence_note: Montage-specific evidence for insomnia is limited; Li et al. 2025 uses HD-tDCS over DMPFC, not this sponge-montage protocol — treat as exploratory.

brain_targets.region: Bilateral Cortex via Ear Clip Electrodes (CES Pathway)
brain_targets.rationale: CES delivers low-level current bilaterally via ear clip electrodes, modulating subcortical arousal systems including the thalamus, limbic system, and reticular activating system. Proposed mechanisms include increased alpha and delta EEG power, modulation of serotonin and noradrenaline, and reduction of cortisol and beta-endorphin alterations associated with insomnia.
brain_targets.qeeg_target: Increase resting alpha (8–12 Hz); reduce pre-sleep beta (20–30 Hz)
brain_targets.evidence_note: Regulatory clearance ≠ robust efficacy evidence for insomnia; see Shekelle et al. 2018 VA systematic review (insufficient evidence for clinically important insomnia effects).

sections.title: Condition Summary
content: Insomnia Disorder: persistent difficulty initiating or maintaining sleep (≥3 nights/week, ≥3 months) with clinically significant daytime impairment.

Prevalence estimates vary by definition; chronic insomnia disorder is often in the single-digit to low–double-digit percent range in surveys — cite primary epidemiology sources when reporting statistics.
Neurobiology: Central hyperarousal (cortical, cognitive, somatic) fails to down-regulate at sleep onset; elevated pre-sleep beta EEG; reduced NREM sleep spindles; disrupted slow-wave sleep.
First-line treatment: CBT-I (Cognitive Behavioural Therapy for Insomnia). Neuromodulation is adjunctive — must document CBT-I offer or completion.
CBT-I remains first-line and must be offered or documented as offered/completed before or alongside any neuromodulation protocol.

sections.title: Patient Selection and Phenotype Identification
content: Confirm DSM-5 insomnia disorder diagnosis: ≥3 months, ≥3 nights/week, daytime impairment documented, primary sleep disorders excluded.
Exclude OSA before treatment — ESS >10 or partner-reported apnoea events require polysomnography referral.
Identify insomnia phenotype: Sleep onset (PHE-INS-01) → CES first-line; Maintenance (PHE-INS-02) → CES or neurofeedback; Hyperarousal (PHE-INS-03) → consider cathodal tDCS right DLPFC.

Screen for psychiatric comorbidities: PHQ-9 and GAD-7 mandatory at baseline. Comorbid anxiety-insomnia → consider adjunctive options with transparent evidence discussion (CES is not a substitute for anxiety treatment).
Document CBT-I history: if not completed, refer for or initiate CBT-I concurrently.

sections.title: Protocol Selection Guide
content: Sleep onset difficulty (PHE-INS-01): discuss PRO-CES-INS-01 as an adjunct only after CBT-I pathway addressed; EV-C evidence for CES per VA 2018 review; device may still be on-label depending on model/jurisdiction.
Maintenance insomnia (PHE-INS-02): CES adjunct may be considered; neurofeedback SMR at Cz (PRO-NF-INS-01) remains EV-C/off-label if CES insufficient or contraindicated.

Hyperarousal insomnia (PHE-INS-03): CES and/or exploratory cathodal R-DLPFC tDCS (PRO-TDCS-INS-01) only with explicit off-label consent and senior clinical judgment — tDCS montage evidence is not interchangeable across studies.
Comorbid anxiety-insomnia (PHE-INS-04): treat anxiety per guideline; neuromodulation adjuncts require dual-condition planning.
Comorbid depression-insomnia (PHE-INS-05): Address depression concurrently. CES or neurofeedback for insomnia; refer to depression protocols for mood treatment.

sections.title: Evidence Summary
content: CES: regulatory clearance exists for some devices; independent systematic review (Shekelle 2018) did not find sufficient evidence for clinically important insomnia effects — document shared decision-making.
Neurofeedback (SMR at Cz): EV-C — Emerging evidence from small controlled studies. Well-grounded mechanistically (SMR-spindle coupling). Off-label. Clinician sign-off required.
tDCS (cathodal right DLPFC): EV-C — Proof-of-concept studies only. Off-label. Restricted to hyperarousal phenotype. Clinician sign-off required.
CBT-I remains the gold standard with the highest evidence grade for insomnia — recommend as first-line or concurrent treatment in all cases.

sections.title: Safety and Governance
content: Document CBT-I offer or referral in every insomnia case before or at the time of neuromodulation protocol generation.
Exclude OSA before initiating any insomnia neuromodulation — ESS screening mandatory.

CES may be on-label for specific devices yet still warrant evidence-informed consent (regulatory status ≠ effect size). Neurofeedback and tDCS require off-label acknowledgement before protocol generation.
tDCS protocols require clinician sign-off and patient export is blocked until sign-off obtained.
Monitor ISI every 5 sessions and sleep diary weekly. Any deterioration triggers clinician review within 48 hours.

sections.title: Monitoring and Escalation
content: Response definition: ≥8-point ISI reduction from baseline; PSQI reduction ≥3 points.
Remission: ISI <8 (sub-threshold range); PSQI ≤5.
Non-response flag: ISI reduction <4 points after 10 sessions — clinician review and protocol reassessment.
Escalation: Worsening ISI from baseline at mid-course — immediate clinician review; consider protocol modification or psychiatric consultation.
Adherence: ≥75% session completion expected. For home CES: missed >5 consecutive nights → clinic contact.

patient_guide.title: Patient Guide: Brain Stimulation for Better Sleep

sections.title: What is Insomnia?
content: Insomnia means having regular difficulty falling asleep, staying asleep, or waking up too early. It is not just an occasional bad night — it is when sleep problems happen most nights and affect how you feel and function during the day.
Insomnia is very common and very treatable. There are effective therapies — both psychological and brain-stimulation-based — that can make a real difference.

sections.title: Your Treatment Options
content: The most effective treatment for insomnia is CBT-I — a structured talking therapy that targets the thoughts and habits that keep insomnia going. Your clinician will discuss this with you.

Some cranial electrotherapy stimulation (CES) devices may be available with regulatory clearance for insomnia in certain countries. Evidence for benefit is mixed; your clinician should explain what is known and unknown before you start.
Neurofeedback is a brain training therapy done in clinic where sensors on your scalp help you train your brain to produce the kind of brain activity associated with good sleep.
These treatments work best together with good sleep habits and, where recommended, CBT-I.

sections.title: What to Expect at Sessions
content: CES: You wear comfortable ear clip electrodes for 60 minutes before bed. Many people find it relaxing. You may notice a mild tingling — this is completely normal and expected.
Neurofeedback: Sessions last about 45 minutes in clinic. Small sensors are placed on your scalp and you watch or listen to feedback showing your brain activity in real time.
Both treatments are non-invasive and painless. They do not involve any injections or sedation.
You will complete a short sleep questionnaire every week or two so your clinician can track your progress.

sections.title: What to Tell Your Clinician
content: Tell your clinician if you notice any skin redness or discomfort at the ear clip sites with CES.
Tell your clinician if your sleep gets worse at any point during treatment — this is important information.
Keep filling in your sleep diary every day — this helps your clinician make the best adjustments to your treatment.
Do not stop any prescribed sleep medication without talking to your doctor first.

technician_sop.title: Technician Standard Operating Procedure: Insomnia CES and Neurofeedback
technician_sop.audience: Trained neuromodulation technician operating under clinician supervision

sections.title: Pre-Session Checklist
content: Confirm patient identity (name + date of birth).
Review most recent session notes and sleep diary for any AEs, parameter adjustments, or clinician flags.
Confirm contraindication screening on file and current (particularly OSA screen and cardiac device status).
For CES: Inspect Alpha-Stim device, ear clip leads, and electrode pads — confirm functional and hygienic.
For neurofeedback: Inspect EEG amplifier, electrodes, gel, and feedback software.

sections.title: CES Electrode Placement
content: Inspect patient ear lobes for any cuts, infection, skin lesions, or active eczema — do not proceed if present.
Remove all metal ear jewellery. Apply conductive solution to felt pads of ear clip electrodes.
Clip ear clip electrodes to bilateral ear lobes. Ensure clips are secure but comfortable.
Connect leads to Alpha-Stim device. Set to 0.5 Hz. Start at minimum intensity (100 µA).
Instruct patient: 'You may feel a mild tingling — this is normal. Tell me if it is uncomfortable and I will adjust.'

sections.title: Neurofeedback Electrode Placement (SMR Protocol)
content: Measure Cz using 10-20 system: midpoint nasion-inion and midpoint left-right preauricular.
Clean Cz with NuPrep abrasive gel. Apply Ten20 conductive paste. Attach electrode, confirm firm contact.
Apply reference electrodes at A1 and A2 (mastoids). Apply ground at Fpz.
Impedance check: all channels must be <5 kΩ before starting. Re-clean if high impedance.
Configure software: reward band SMR 12–15 Hz; inhibit band theta 4–8 Hz. Set adaptive threshold.

sections.title: During Session Monitoring
content: CES: Check patient comfort at 5 minutes and 30 minutes. Confirm ear clips remain in position.
Neurofeedback: Monitor signal quality and artefact level every 5 minutes. Remind patient to minimise movement.
Log any reported sensations (tingling, drowsiness, headache) in real time.
For neurofeedback: brief drowsiness post-session is expected — advise against driving immediately after late-day sessions.

sections.title: Post-Session Documentation
content: Remove electrodes. Inspect ear lobes (CES) or scalp sites (neurofeedback) for any redness or skin reaction.
Administer ISI if session falls on a monitoring point.
Ask patient: 'How is your sleep going? Any changes since last session? Any side effects?'
Log session parameters, duration, reward rate (NF), and any AEs in session record.
Flag any AEs to supervising clinician within 1 hour.

assessment_schedule.timing: Before session 1 (baseline); every 5 sessions during treatment; end of course
2 weeks before session 1 (baseline); daily throughout treatment course; 2 weeks post-course (follow-up)

assessment_schedule.frequency: Once at baseline and once at course completion

assessment_schedule.timing: Baseline, mid-course (session 10), and end of course

assessment_schedule.frequency: Once at baseline and once at course completion

response_thresholds.response_threshold: ≥8-point reduction from baseline ISI score (Morin et al., 2011 MCID for ISI)
response_thresholds.non_response_threshold: ISI reduction <4 points after 10 sessions — triggers clinician review and protocol reassessment
response_thresholds.deterioration_threshold: ISI score increase ≥4 points from baseline at any monitoring point — immediate clinician review

response_thresholds.response_threshold: ≥3-point total score reduction from baseline PSQI
response_thresholds.non_response_threshold: No PSQI improvement at end of course — triggers review
response_thresholds.deterioration_threshold: PSQI increase ≥3 points from baseline — clinician review required

adverse_event_triggers.event_type: Skin burn or open wound at electrode site
adverse_event_triggers.action: Cease stimulation immediately. Photograph and document lesion. Refer for medical review. Do not resume until healed and electrode technique reviewed.

adverse_event_triggers.event_type: Seizure during or within 2 hours of session
adverse_event_triggers.action: Call emergency services. Do not resume neuromodulation without neurology clearance. Incident report within 24 hours.

adverse_event_triggers.event_type: New or worsening suicidal ideation (PHQ-9 item 9 score ≥1)
adverse_event_triggers.action: Same-day clinician review. Complete suicide risk assessment. Suspend treatment until safety review complete.
Clinician review within 2 working days. Assess for new stressor, medication change, or protocol mismatch. Consider protocol modification.
Reduce stimulation intensity (tDCS to 1 mA; CES to minimum) or session length. Clinician review if persists after 2 consecutive sessions.

adverse_event_triggers.event_type: Skin redness at ear clip site (CES)
adverse_event_triggers.action: Inspect site. If intact skin: apply moisturising barrier cream, check electrode pad hydration, reduce current. If skin broken: cease CES, medical review.

escalation_rules.action: Clinician review: reassess phenotype match, review CBT-I engagement, consider adjunct modality (add neurofeedback to CES, or vice versa), assess for emerging comorbidity

escalation_rules.trigger: ISI score increases ≥4 points from baseline at any monitoring point
escalation_rules.action: Urgent clinician review within 48 hours. Assess for new stressor, medication change, worsening comorbid mood disorder

escalation_rules.trigger: OSA suspected (ESS >10 or new reports of snoring/apnoeas)
escalation_rules.action: Suspend insomnia-specific neuromodulation pending OSA assessment. Refer for polysomnography. Resume insomnia protocols only after OSA addressed

escalation_rules.trigger: Three or more missed home CES sessions consecutively
escalation_rules.action: Clinic contact within 48 hours. Assess adherence barriers. Clinician review if pattern continues
Immediate treatment suspension. Clinical lead notification within 2 hours. Incident report within 24 hours

adherence_rules.action_on_low_adherence: Clinic contact within 48 hours. Document reason for missed sessions. Address barriers (home device technical issues, motivation, schedule). Clinician review if adherence <60% without clear justification.

report_templates.name: Insomnia Neuromodulation Intake Summary
report_templates.frequency: Once — at intake before first session

sections.title: Patient and Diagnosis Summary
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Confirm insomnia disorder diagnosis, duration, frequency, and phenotype (onset, maintenance, hyperarousal, comorbid). Note any diagnostic uncertainties.

sections.title: Baseline Sleep Scores
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Comment on baseline insomnia severity and quality. Note daytime functioning impact and comorbid anxiety/depression scores.

sections.title: CBT-I and Prior Treatment History
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Document CBT-I history (offered, completed, response). Note prior sleep medications and response. State rationale for adding neuromodulation at this stage.

sections.title: OSA and Primary Sleep Disorder Exclusion
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Confirm primary sleep disorders have been excluded or are being managed concurrently. Document polysomnography result if available.

sections.title: Contraindication Review and Protocol Selection
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Confirm contraindications excluded. State selected protocol and rationale including phenotype match. Note on-label vs off-label status and consent obtained.

sections.title: Session Adherence
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Comment on attendance pattern. Note any missed sessions and home CES compliance (if applicable).

sections.title: Sleep Score Trajectory
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Describe ISI trajectory from baseline. State whether response threshold is on track. Review sleep diary data for objective trends.

sections.title: Adverse Events Since Last Review
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Summarise any AEs logged (skin reactions, headache, worsened insomnia). State actions taken.

sections.title: Protocol Adjustments
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Document any protocol parameter changes, reason, and expected impact.

sections.title: Treatment Summary
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Summarise the treatment course delivered, including modality, protocol, and adherence.

sections.title: Outcome Scores — Baseline to Endpoint
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Interpret outcome data. State response status (responder/non-responder/remission). Comment on sleep diary objective data changes.

sections.title: Safety and Adverse Events Summary
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Confirm all AEs documented and resolved. State any ongoing concerns.

sections.title: Recommendations
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: State recommendation: maintenance CES, repeat neurofeedback course, CBT-I referral, sleep medication review, or discharge. Specify follow-up assessment timing.

sections.title: Subjective
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Document patient-reported sleep quality since last session, sleep diary entries, medication adherence, and any sleep-related concerns.

sections.title: Objective
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Record protocol delivered, parameters used, session duration, and any technician observations.

sections.title: Assessment
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Clinical impression of sleep trajectory. Note any changes in insomnia severity, comorbid mood, or risk factors.

sections.title: Plan
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: State plan for next session including parameter adjustments, additional assessments, or referral actions.

home_program_templates.name: Insomnia Home CES Programme — Alpha-Stim
patient_instructions: Use the Alpha-Stim device each evening, within 1 hour of your planned bedtime.
Find a quiet, comfortable place to sit or lie down. Dim the lights. Avoid screens during your session.
Moisten the felt pads on the ear clip electrodes with the conductive solution provided. Clip both ear clips to your ear lobes.
Turn the device on and set the frequency to 0.5 Hz. Start at the lowest current setting (100 µA).
You may feel a very mild tingling — this is normal. If uncomfortable, reduce the current slightly.
Sit quietly for 60 minutes. You may listen to gentle music or practise slow breathing.
After the session, record your session in your sleep diary: time started, comfort rating (1–10), and how you feel.
Do not use the device if you feel unwell, have an active ear infection, or have removed a pacemaker or similar device.
safety_checklist: My ear lobes are free from cuts, sores, or active skin conditions.
I do not have a pacemaker or implanted cardiac device.
I have removed all metal ear jewellery.
I feel well enough to use the device tonight.
I am not planning to drive or operate machinery within 30 minutes of my session.
reporting_requirements: Complete sleep diary entry every morning (bedtime, time to fall asleep, awakenings, wake time, sleep quality 1–10).
Log CES session time and comfort rating in your daily record.
Contact the clinic if you experience skin redness at ear clip sites lasting more than 1 hour.
Email or call clinic if you miss 3 or more consecutive sessions.
Bring your sleep diary to every clinic appointment for review.
contraindications_for_home: Active ear infection or open wound at ear lobe site — do not use until healed
Feeling acutely unwell, feverish, or in a mental health crisis
After consuming alcohol (>1 unit) — postpone session
If your doctor has added a new medication that may interact with electrical stimulation — contact clinic first
home_program_templates.evidence_note: Home use must follow manufacturer IFU and local prescribing rules. Independent systematic review did not establish robust insomnia efficacy for CES — track outcomes with sleep diary/ISI.

consent_documents.name: Insomnia Neuromodulation — General Informed Consent
consent_documents.when_required: Before first session of any neuromodulation treatment for insomnia
key_disclosures: Some CES devices may carry regulatory clearance for insomnia where I live; clearance reflects device marketing authorization, not guaranteed benefit. Neurofeedback and tDCS for insomnia are typically off-label relative to specific device indications.
CBT-I (Cognitive Behavioural Therapy for Insomnia) is the first-line evidence-based treatment. I have been informed of this option and it has been offered to me.
Results are not guaranteed and vary between individuals. Some patients experience no meaningful benefit.
Common side effects of CES include mild tingling at ear clip sites and occasional headache. Neurofeedback may cause brief post-session drowsiness. tDCS may cause tingling or skin redness.
Serious but rare risks include seizure (all electrical stimulation modalities), skin irritation or burn at electrode sites (CES, tDCS), and transient mood changes.
I understand that neuromodulation is typically adjunctive to CBT-I and lifestyle modification — not a standalone treatment.
I understand I may withdraw from treatment at any time without penalty.
My treatment data will be stored securely and used to monitor my response. It will not be shared without my consent except where legally required.
I have been informed of alternative treatment options and had the opportunity to ask questions.

consent_documents.name: Insomnia Off-Label Treatment Acknowledgement (Neurofeedback / tDCS)
consent_documents.when_required: Before protocol generation for neurofeedback (PRO-NF-INS-01) or tDCS (PRO-TDCS-INS-01) insomnia protocols
key_disclosures: The proposed treatment (neurofeedback or tDCS) is being used off-label for insomnia — it is not specifically approved by the FDA or equivalent regulatory authority for this indication.
My clinician has explained the current evidence base for this treatment in the context of my insomnia diagnosis.
I understand the evidence grade applicable to my proposed protocol (EV-C — emerging evidence from controlled studies).

I consent to proceeding with this off-label treatment after consideration of the available evidence and alternatives (including CBT-I and, where discussed, adjunctive CES with transparent review of systematic evidence).
My clinician will monitor my response and adjust or discontinue treatment if it is not beneficial or if risks emerge.
consent_documents.review_frequency: Once per modality per treatment course

consent_documents.name: Insomnia CES Home Device Agreement — Alpha-Stim
consent_documents.when_required: Before Alpha-Stim device is prescribed or loaned for home use
key_disclosures: The Alpha-Stim AID device is FDA-cleared for insomnia and I have been trained in its safe use.
I will use the device only as prescribed — 60 minutes nightly before bed at 0.5 Hz.
I will not use the device if I have an active ear infection, have removed a cardiac device, or feel acutely unwell.
I will complete my sleep diary daily and report any side effects to the clinic promptly.
If the device is loaned by the clinic, it remains clinic property and must be returned in good condition.
I will not lend the device to others or use it for purposes other than my prescribed insomnia programme.
consent_documents.review_frequency: Once; updated if device is changed

consent_documents.name: Insomnia Contraindication Screening Form
consent_documents.when_required: Before first neuromodulation session and at start of any new treatment course
key_disclosures: I have disclosed all relevant medical conditions including seizure disorder, cardiac device implants, ear conditions, and skin conditions at proposed electrode sites.
I have disclosed all current medications including sleep medications, antidepressants, and cardiac medications.
I have been screened for obstructive sleep apnoea and primary sleep disorders.
I understand that if my medical status changes during treatment, I must inform the clinic before the next session.
My responses on this screening form are truthful and complete to the best of my knowledge.
consent_documents.review_frequency: Before each new treatment course and at 6-month intervals for ongoing treatment
